text:
FOUNDATION ONE CDx COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SPECIMEN AND ORDERING INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Patient Name: Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
Ordering Physician: [Oncology / Gastrointestinal Oncology Service]
Facility: [Institution on file]

Specimen ID: [LAB-ACCESSION ON FILE]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue
Source: Colonoscopic biopsy, sigmoid colon mass
Date of Procedure: [see clinical records]
Date Received at Laboratory: [on file]
Date Report Issued: [on file]

Diagnosis (Submitted): Adenocarcinoma, sigmoid colon, metastatic (Stage IV, M1a). Synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. KRAS mutant per hotspot testing.
Prior Treatment at Time of Biopsy: None (diagnostic biopsy, treatment-naive specimen).
Tumor Content (estimated): 60% (adequate for comprehensive genomic profiling).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
METHODOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Platform: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Cambridge, MA). FDA-approved comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) assay.

Genomic Targets: Hybrid-capture-based next-generation sequencing (NGS) of the entire coding regions of 324 cancer-relevant genes, select intronic regions for rearrangement detection, and whole-genome copy number analysis from the same library. Includes analysis of short variants (substitutions, insertions/deletions), copy number alterations (amplifications and homozygous deletions), structural rearrangements/gene fusions, and whole-genome-derived genomic signatures.

Analysis Includes:
- Short variants: substitutions, insertions/deletions in 324 genes
- Copy number alterations: amplifications and homozygous deletions
- Rearrangements/fusions: select introns of 36 genes
- Tumor mutational burden (TMB): estimated from somatic synonymous and non-synonymous mutations across coding regions
- Microsatellite instability (MSI): assessment of 95 intronic homopolymer repeat loci
- Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): genome-wide assessment

Sequencing Depth: Mean coverage >500x across all targeted regions.
Sensitivity: SNVs/indels ≥5% allele frequency in specimens with ≥20% tumor content; copy number amplifications and homozygous deletions from whole-genome analysis; fusions from targeted RNA-seq for select genes.
Specificity: >99% for SNVs, validated per CAP/CLIA-accredited laboratory standards.

This assay is performed in a CAP-accredited, CLIA-certified laboratory. Results should be interpreted in conjunction with clinical and pathologic findings by a qualified physician.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB): 5 mutations/Mb
  Classification: LOW (Threshold for TMB-High: ≥10 mut/Mb per FDA companion diagnostic criteria)
  Clinical Implication: Low TMB; pembrolizumab approval for TMB-High (≥10 mut/Mb) tumor-agnostic indication does not apply.

MICROSATELLITE STATUS (MSI): MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Method: Analysis of 95 intronic homopolymer loci; 0/95 loci demonstrating instability.
  Clinical Implication: MSS status indicates the tumor is not a candidate for immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy based on mismatch repair deficiency / MSI-High status. Consistent with IHC findings of retained MMR protein expression (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+) reported on diagnostic pathology.

GENOMIC LOSS OF HETEROZYGOSITY (gLOH): 14%
  Classification: INTERMEDIATE (Low <10%; High ≥16% threshold varies by indication)
  Note: gLOH is primarily clinically relevant for BRCA-associated cancers (ovarian, breast, prostate) in the context of PARP inhibitor sensitivity. In colorectal adenocarcinoma, gLOH has no established actionable implication at this level.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DETAILED GENOMIC ALTERATIONS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

** SECTION A: CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT / ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS **

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A)
   Gene/Pathway: KRAS / RAS-MAPK signaling pathway
   Altele Frequency: 42%
   Copy Number Context: Diploid (CN 2.0)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Known oncogenic driver mutation; included in OncoKB Level 1 evidence (FDA-recognized biomarker for anti-EGFR therapy exclusion in colorectal cancer)
   Functional Impact: Activating missense substitution at codon 12 (glycine to aspartate). Constitutively activates downstream RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK signaling, bypassing upstream EGFR receptor control. One of the most common oncogenic drivers in colorectal cancer, occurring in approximately 35-40% of cases.
   Actionability:
     - EXCLUDES benefit from anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody therapies (cetuximab [Erbitux], panitumumab [Vectibix]) per NCCN Category 1 evidence and FDA labeling. RAS mutations (KRAS/NRAS exon 2/3/4) are mandatory biomarker exclusions for anti-EGFR therapy in mCRC.
     - KRAS G12D is also a potential target for novel KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133, investigational) currently in early-phase clinical trials; no FDA-approved KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor available at this time.
     - SOS1 inhibitors and SHP2 inhibitors targeting upstream RAS signaling are under investigation but remain experimental for this mutation in CRC.
   Allele frequency comment: AF 42% is consistent with a heterozygous mutation in tumor cells comprising approximately 60% of the specimen, suggesting dominant clonal presence.

2. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Gene/Pathway: TP53 / Cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, DNA damage response
   Allele Frequency: 48%
   Copy Number Context: Likely loss of heterozygosity (AF consistent with LOH at TP53 locus)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Loss-of-function mutation; one of the most common TP53 hotspot mutations in human cancer
   Functional Impact: Arginine 248 is a critical DNA contact residue in the p53 DNA-binding domain. The R248W substitution abrogates sequence-specific DNA binding and abolishes transcriptional activation of p53 target genes (p21, BAX, PUMA, MDM2). This variant also exerts dominant-negative activity, inhibiting residual wild-type p53 from co-expressed heteromers. Loss of TP53 function impairs G1/S cell cycle checkpoint, promotes genomic instability, and enables survival under oncogenic stress.
   Actionability: No approved targeted therapies directly targeting TP53 loss-of-function in colorectal cancer. APR-246 (eprenetapopt), a TP53 reactivator, has been investigated primarily in hematologic malignancies and limited solid tumor settings; not standard of care. High allele frequency (48%) suggests clonal dominance and/or LOH, consistent with its role as an early event in colorectal carcinogenesis (APC --> KRAS --> SMAD4/TP53 progression model).

3. APC p.E1554* (c.4660G>T)
   Gene/Pathway: APC / WNT-beta-catenin signaling
   Allele Frequency: 51%
   Copy Number Context: Consistent with loss of heterozygosity
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Truncating nonsense mutation; loss of tumor suppressor function
   Functional Impact: APC (adenomatous polyposis coli) encodes a central negative regulator of the WNT/beta-catenin pathway. The nonsense mutation at E1554 produces a truncated protein lacking the armadillo repeat domains, SAMP motifs, and the basic domain, effectively abolishing beta-catenin degradation complex function. This leads to constitutive nuclear accumulation of beta-catenin and transcriptional activation of WNT target genes (MYC, CCND1, MMP7). APC truncating mutations are the initiating driver event in the vast majority of sporadic colorectal cancers (present in ~85% of CRC). The high AF (51%) with apparent LOH is consistent with biallelic inactivation (somatic two-hit model).
   Actionability: No approved targeted therapies for APC loss. Investigational approaches include beta-catenin / TCF inhibitors (still early-phase). Not actionable with currently approved agents.

** SECTION B: ADDITIONAL GENOMIC ALTERATIONS OF POTENTIAL SIGNIFICANCE **

4. SMAD4 p.R361H (c.1082G>A)
   Gene/Pathway: SMAD4 / TGF-beta signaling
   Allele Frequency: 38%
   Copy Number Context: Chromosome 18q loss (see copy number section below)
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC -- Missense mutation at conserved residue in MH2 domain; loss-of-function predicted
   Functional Impact: SMAD4 (DPC4) is a central mediator of TGF-beta tumor-suppressive signaling. The R361H substitution disrupts the MH2 domain interface required for heteromeric SMAD complex formation and nuclear transcriptional co-activation. SMAD4 loss in CRC is associated with accelerated metastatic progression, resistance to TGF-beta-mediated growth inhibition, and worse clinical outcomes in metastatic disease. SMAD4 loss has been reported in approximately 35% of mCRC cases and is enriched in liver-metastatic disease. Biallelic inactivation is supported by coincident 18q chromosomal loss.
   Prognostic Note: SMAD4 mutation/loss is associated with worse prognosis in Stage IV CRC and has been correlated with shorter progression-free survival on first-line chemotherapy in some series. It may predict for early distant metastasis (consistent with this patient's synchronous liver metastases at diagnosis).
   Actionability: No approved targeted therapies for SMAD4 loss in CRC. SMAD4 loss does not predict response to anti-EGFR therapy beyond the dominant RAS exclusion. Under investigation as a marker for response to combined TGF-beta pathway inhibition with immune checkpoint inhibitors (not relevant given MSS status here).

5. CDKN2A -- Homozygous Deletion
   Gene/Pathway: CDKN2A (p16-INK4a / p14-ARF) / Cell cycle regulation (CDK4/6-Rb axis)
   Copy Number: Homozygous deletion (CN ~0), chromosome 9p21 locus
   Classification: PATHOGENIC -- Complete loss of tumor suppressor function
   Functional Impact: CDKN2A encodes two distinct tumor suppressor proteins via alternative reading frames: p16-INK4a (inhibits CDK4/6, preventing Rb phosphorylation and G1/S entry) and p14-ARF (stabilizes p53 by inhibiting MDM2-mediated ubiquitination). Homozygous deletion abrogates both cell cycle and p53-regulatory tumor suppressive functions. In the context of pre-existing TP53 R248W mutation, the p14-ARF loss may be redundant for p53 pathway disruption, but p16 loss enables unchecked CDK4/6-mediated Rb inactivation. CDKN2A deletion occurs in approximately 20-25% of colorectal cancers.
   Actionability: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are not approved in CRC and clinical trial data in CRC have been disappointing, likely due to context-dependent Rb pathway complexity. No standard actionable implication in this disease type.

6. FBXW7 p.R505C (c.1513C>T)
   Gene/Pathway: FBXW7 / Ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis, MYC/NOTCH/CCNE1 regulation
   Allele Frequency: 22%
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC -- Recurrently mutated residue (hotspot R465, R479, R505 in WD40 domain)
   Functional Impact: FBXW7 encodes the substrate recognition subunit of the SCF-FBXW7 E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, responsible for targeted degradation of key oncoproteins including MYC, NOTCH1/2, CCNE1, and JUN. The R505C hotspot mutation disrupts substrate recognition, impairing ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of these oncoproteins. Loss of FBXW7 function stabilizes MYC protein and activates NOTCH signaling. The relatively lower allele frequency (22%) may suggest subclonal presence or admixture with normal tissue.
   Actionability: No directly approved therapies targeting FBXW7 loss. MYC protein stabilization resulting from FBXW7 loss may contribute to the concurrent MYC locus gain observed in this tumor (see copy number alterations). Gamma-secretase inhibitors targeting NOTCH have not proven effective in unselected CRC. No standard actionable implication.

** SECTION C: GENES WITH CONFIRMED WILD-TYPE / NEGATIVE STATUS (CLINICALLY RELEVANT) **

- KRAS exon 3 (codon 59, 61): Wild-type
- KRAS exon 4 (codon 117, 146): Wild-type
- NRAS exon 2 (codon 12, 13): Wild-type
- NRAS exon 3 (codon 59, 61): Wild-type
- NRAS exon 4 (codon 117, 146): Wild-type
- BRAF p.V600E: NOT DETECTED
- BRAF non-V600 mutations: NOT DETECTED
- ERBB2 (HER2): NOT AMPLIFIED (copy number 2.1; amplification threshold CN ≥6 or ratio ≥2.0)
- ERBB2 activating mutations (exon 20): NOT DETECTED
- PIK3CA exon 9 (E545K, E542K): NOT DETECTED
- PIK3CA exon 20 (H1047R): NOT DETECTED
- MET: NOT AMPLIFIED (CN 1.9)
- NTRK1 fusion: NOT DETECTED
- NTRK2 fusion: NOT DETECTED
- NTRK3 fusion: NOT DETECTED
- RET fusion: NOT DETECTED
- ALK fusion: NOT DETECTED
- ROS1 fusion: NOT DETECTED
- POLE: Wild-type (no pathogenic mutations in exonuclease domain)
- POLD1: Wild-type
- BRCA1: Wild-type
- BRCA2: Wild-type
- MLH1: Wild-type (no somatic mutation detected; consistent with protein retention by IHC)
- MSH2: Wild-type
- MSH6: Wild-type
- PMS2: Wild-type

** SECTION D: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS **

- Chromosome 8q gain (MYC locus, 8q24.21): Copy number ~4.1 (low-level gain). Consistent with MYC pathway activation; seen in approximately 60% of colorectal cancers with left-sided primary. Cooperates with FBXW7 loss.
- Chromosome 18q loss (SMAD2/SMAD4 locus, 18q21): Copy number ~1.0 (hemizygous loss). Supports biallelic SMAD4 inactivation in conjunction with SMAD4 R361H point mutation. Chromosome 18q loss is one of the most common somatic alterations in CRC (~70% of cases).
- Chromosome 20q gain (20q13 region; includes AURKA, ZNF217): Low-level gain (CN ~3.2). Chromosomal gain at 20q is common in CRC and may contribute to cell cycle dysregulation via AURKA overexpression.
- CDKN2A locus (9p21.3): Homozygous deletion confirmed (CN ~0) as described above.

** SECTION E: GENE FUSIONS AND REARRANGEMENTS **

No gene fusions or structural rearrangements detected in the 36 genes assessed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERPRETATION AND CLINICAL SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This comprehensive genomic profiling result is consistent with the known molecular landscape of sporadic left-sided colorectal adenocarcinoma. The dominant molecular profile is characterized by the classical adenoma-to-carcinoma progression pathway: APC truncating mutation (initiating event) --> KRAS activating mutation (early progression) --> SMAD4/TP53 loss (late progression events), consistent with the canonical Fearon-Vogelstein model of colorectal carcinogenesis. The tumor harbors high-frequency clonal mutations in all four of these genes, suggesting a well-established dominant clone.

The KRAS G12D mutation is the most clinically impactful finding: it is a dominant oncogenic driver that definitively excludes this patient from treatment with anti-EGFR antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab), which would otherwise be considered for RAS/BRAF wild-type left-sided mCRC. There are no currently FDA-approved KRAS G12D-targeted agents for colorectal cancer.

The MSS / pMMR status, confirmed by both this genomic assay and diagnostic IHC, indicates the tumor is not expected to respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1/PD-L1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) or CTLA-4. The low TMB (5 mut/Mb) further supports this conclusion.

SMAD4 R361H loss, combined with 18q chromosomal deletion, represents a biallelic inactivation event with potential prognostic significance in stage IV disease. Published data suggest SMAD4-mutant mCRC may have a more aggressive metastatic phenotype (consistent with synchronous bilobar liver involvement in this patient), though this does not alter first-line therapeutic strategy.

The CDKN2A homozygous deletion and FBXW7 R505C mutation are recurrent alterations in CRC with no currently approved actionable implications.

No targetable alterations have been identified that would guide use of molecularly matched therapies beyond standard chemotherapy backbones. Specifically: no HER2 amplification (excludes trastuzumab/pertuzumab or tucatinib), no NTRK fusion (excludes larotrectinib/entrectinib), no BRAF V600E (excludes BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations), no RET fusion, no ALK/ROS1 fusion, no BRCA1/2 mutation (no PARP inhibitor indication).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ACTIONABILITY TIER SUMMARY (OncoKB / AMP/ASCO/CAP Framework)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

TIER I -- FDA-APPROVED THERAPY IN THIS TUMOR TYPE (Directly Actionable):
  KRAS p.G12D (exon 2): Anti-EGFR therapy EXCLUDED (cetuximab, panitumumab contraindicated).
    Evidence: FDA-approved companion diagnostic; NCCN Category 1 recommendation.
    Action: Do NOT administer cetuximab or panitumumab.

TIER II -- INVESTIGATIONAL / CLINICAL TRIAL OPPORTUNITY:
  KRAS p.G12D: Eligibility for KRAS G12D-targeted inhibitor clinical trials (e.g., MRTX1133 and combination studies). Patient should be referred to available trials at progression or as appropriate.
  SMAD4 loss + MSS: Emerging interest in combinatorial strategies; no active trials at immediate standard.

TIER III -- PROGNOSTIC / BIOLOGICAL SIGNIFICANCE (Not Directly Actionable):
  SMAD4 p.R361H + 18q loss: Associated with aggressive metastatic biology in mCRC; may portend shorter progression-free survival.
  CDKN2A homozygous deletion: Loss of cell cycle regulation; no approved CDK4/6 inhibitor strategy in CRC.
  FBXW7 p.R505C: Subclonal; contributes to MYC stabilization; no approved targeted therapy.
  Chromosome 8q gain (MYC): Genomic background alteration; no approved MYC-directed therapy.
  Low TMB / MSS: Confirms lack of immunotherapy eligibility.

TIER IV -- BENIGN / VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE:
  No variants of uncertain significance (VUS) reported in this specimen.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SIGNATURE FINDINGS INTERPRETATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The mutational landscape of this tumor is consistent with the CRC chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway, characterized by: aneuploidy (chromosomal gains and losses), dominant APC/KRAS/TP53/SMAD4 co-mutations, low TMB, MSS phenotype, and absence of POLE/POLD1 exonuclease mutations. This is the most common molecular subtype of colorectal cancer (approximately 65-70% of cases) and is distinct from the hypermutated MSI-H/TMB-High subtype (approximately 15% of cases) and the POLE-ultramutated subtype (<5%). The CIN pathway tumors have historically lower response rates to immune checkpoint inhibition and derive their primary therapeutic benefit from chemotherapy-based regimens.

The gLOH of 14% is intermediate and, in the context of wild-type BRCA1/2 and absence of HRD-associated mutational signature, does not suggest a homologous recombination deficiency phenotype amenable to PARP inhibition.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
REPORT ATTESTATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This report was generated and reviewed by the Foundation Medicine molecular pathology team. Results have been confirmed by a board-certified molecular pathologist. This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine, Inc. It has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. The FDA has determined that such clearance or approval is not necessary. This laboratory is certified under the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 (CLIA) as qualified to perform high-complexity clinical laboratory testing.

Interpreting Pathologist: [Molecular Pathologist, MD/PhD -- Foundation Medicine]
Report Status: FINAL

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

5. Brosens LAA et al. SMAD4 loss in colorectal cancer: clinical and molecular associations and prognosis. Histopathology. 2015.
